items.name: Files.md
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Files.md – Open-source alternative to Obsidian
items.summary: Local-first markdown notes app with Obsidian-like links and file-based storage.
items.why_included: Commenters praised the clean local-files workflow and said they were moving back toward text-editor-plus-git notes.
items.evidence: One commenter said the app looked 'really slick'; others praised the local-files model and the plain text workflow.

items.name: markdown-oxide
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Files.md – Open-source alternative to Obsidian
items.summary: Rust markdown LSP that adds notes features to Helix and other editors.
items.why_included: It was repeatedly recommended as the lightweight way to get Obsidian-style linking without a heavy app.
items.evidence: A commenter explicitly recommended Helix with the markdown-oxide LSP, and another said they'd give it a try.

items.name: Syncthing
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Files.md – Open-source alternative to Obsidian
items.summary: Peer-to-peer file sync tool for keeping local notes in sync across devices.
items.why_included: Commenters singled it out as the free sync layer that made note-taking work without cloud lock-in.
items.evidence: One user said they chose Syncthing for note syncing and that it 'works flawlessly'.

items.name: Vaultwarden
items.why_included: Multiple commenters reported years of reliable self-hosting and explicitly recommended it as the Bitwarden alternative.
items.evidence: Users said they had run Vaultwarden for 5+ to 7+ years with no issues, and one advised self-hosting a Vaultwarden instance.

items.name: KeepassXC
items.summary: Cross-platform password manager favored by commenters after leaving Bitwarden.
items.why_included: It surfaced as a practical offline-friendly alternative with desktop, mobile, and browser support.
items.evidence: One commenter said they moved to KeepassXC on desktop plus KeepassDX on Android, and another called the KeePass ecosystem a fantastic advert.

items.name: Level1Techs KVM
items.source_story_title: Two computers, one monitor, zero fiddling (2025)
items.summary: Dual-monitor hardware KVM switch for painless multi-computer desk switching.
items.why_included: Commenters treated it as the clean hardware fix after software switching and monitor-input juggling failed.
items.evidence: A user said the Level1Techs KVM solved the multi-computer/multi-monitor problem, removed switching lag, and was worth the price.

items.name: Haiku OS
items.source_story_title: Haiku OS runs on M1 Macs now
items.summary: BeOS-inspired operating system now running on M1 Macs.
items.why_included: Comments framed it as fast, surprisingly stable, and still fun to use on old hardware.
items.evidence: One commenter reported Haiku as fast and surprisingly stable on a ThinkPad X40, with Emacs and VLC working well.

items.name: 180db
items.source_story_title: Show HN: We missed Winamp, so we built an audio player for macOS
items.summary: macOS audio player built for people who miss the Winamp era.
items.why_included: The thread drew praise from commenters who wanted a local-library music player with more personality.
items.evidence: A commenter said they tried the linked app and that it 'definitely has a vibe to it'.

items.name: Stainless
items.summary: API and SDK generation platform for developer interfaces.
items.why_included: Commenters called it a fantastic product and described it as important infrastructure for SDKs and MCP servers.
items.evidence: HN users described Stainless as a fantastic product and said many companies rely on it to generate SDKs, CLIs, and MCP servers.

items.name: Qwen 3.7 Preview
items.summary: Preview of Qwen’s next open-weight model family.
items.why_included: Comments showed strong enthusiasm for the Qwen 3.x line as fast, capable, and useful at home.
items.evidence: Replies called Qwen 3.6 amazing, fast, and standard open weights for everyday use, which carried the thread’s excitement for the preview.
